chunk_text: Intelligence is a joint function that serves to provide information, assessments, and estimates to the commanders and their staff to support situational understanding and enable decision-making. Global Integrated Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance (GIISR) is one of the Air Force’s Core Functions. ISR is an activity that synchronizes and integrates the planning and operation of sensors, assets, processing, exploitation, and dissemination (PED) systems, and analytic systems or capabilities directly support current and future intelligence operations. The Air Force Deputy Chief of Staff for Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance (AF/A2) is responsible for policy formulation, planning, evaluation, oversight, and leadership of United States Air Force (USAF) global integrated ISR. As the USAF’s Senior Intelligence Officer (SIO), the AF/A2 has the authority to collaborate and coordinate with the Under Secretary of War for Intelligence and Security [USW(I&S)] and execute, as delegated, the Secretary of the Air Force’s Secretary of the Military Department and Department of War Component Head intelligence responsibilities.

chunk_text: United States Air Force (USAF) intelligence efforts are guided by a clear hierarchy, flowing from national strategic policy and guidance down to daily operations. The foundation of top-level direction from national security, defense, and intelligence community (IC) standards shapes the organization and prioritization of intelligence forces. From there, this strategic intent is translated into operational practice through specific USAF core functions, such as Global Integrated Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance (GIISR). Intelligence production follows a structured progression, moving from raw data and basic information toward refined assessments and enduring knowledge frameworks. Understanding this progression highlights the essential difference between intelligence as a capability (the processes, people, and systems) and intelligence as a product (the outputs that inform commanders’ decisions). Together, these elements form an integrated architecture that aligns national priorities with the execution of the joint intelligence process, ensuring coherence from strategic guidance to tactical application.

chunk_text: The President of the United States (US) and the Secretary of War (SecWar) provide strategic guidance for the Department of War (DoW), which directs the prioritization of homeland defense and deterring threats in key regions such as the Indo-Pacific. These strategies create a cascading framework of objectives, priorities, and requirements that flow from the highest levels of national leadership down to the individual analyst and intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) platform. This alignment ensures a unified effort of national and military intelligence for military operations, linking operational intelligence collection and analysis directly to the nation's strategic objectives. National Intelligence Strategy (NIS). The Director of National Intelligence’s (DNI) strategy calls for a more agile, integrated IC that collaborates across the 18 member agencies and with partners, reinforcing the need for USAF intelligence activities to share data with other services, combatant commands (CCMD), and multinational intelligence partners to inform national decision-making. This emphasizes common standards, data interoperability, and fusion intelligence efforts.

chunk_text: The production of intelligence at the speed of modern conflict is no longer a matter of simply collecting more data, but of mastering the data collected. A deliberate data governance strategy is therefore a prerequisite for any effective intelligence operation. Without it, the immense volume of information from the operating environment remains a chaotic liability rather than a strategic asset. The ultimate goal is to achieve decision advantage, which is the ability to make better, faster, and more effective decisions than an adversary. It is gained by accessing superior information, analyzing it more effectively, and acting upon it more decisively. USAF A2’s ISR Strategic Vision 2032 USAF A2’s ISR Strategic Vision 2032 describes how AF ISR will operate in 2032, with a focus on executing the six Air Force Future Operating Concept (AFFOC) fights, and provides guidance on the strategic resourcing, technology, and human capital choices required to design and develop those forces. AF ISR will provide vital, modern, context-relevant capabilities as a core function of the USAF.

section: MASTERING THE DATA-RICH BATTLESPACE FOR DECISION ADVANTAGE
chunk_text: To achieve a decisive edge, military operations aim for decision advantage. This advantage is built on a foundation of information advantage, the culmination of a critical process that transforms raw data into actionable intelligence. Initially, data exists as discrete, unorganized facts such as raw signal intercepts or satellite images. When this data is processed, organized, and given context, it becomes information. Intelligence represents the final, most valuable stage of this progression. It is the product of analyzing information to understand its significance, predict future actions, and identify vulnerabilities. By effectively converting data into information and information into intelligence, a force gains a superior understanding of the OE, enabling the rapid and informed decision-making that is the hallmark of decision advantage.

chunk_text: Mastering the data-rich environment begins with disciplined organization and standardization at scale. The USAF relies on a structured framework built on taxonomies, ontologies, and knowledge models, which refine raw data into usable intelligence. These components create a common language that enables humans and machines to rapidly interpret, relate, and analyze complex information, accelerating the transformation of data into decision-quality insight. Proper governance ensures data is discoverable, accessible, and trustworthy, enabling rapid synthesis of intelligence and providing commanders with the sustained decision advantage required to fight and win inside information-dense environments. These distinctions highlight the importance of structured data governance and analytic frameworks. The USAF intelligence enterprise contributes to the IC by aligning with the Defense All Source Analytic Enterprise (DIAAE) knowledge model for object-based intelligence (OBI) and production (OBP).

chunk_text: Object-Based Intelligence. OBI is an approach to intelligence analysis that focuses on defining and analyzing specific objects of interest to understand their relationships, vulnerabilities, and potential impact. It is a shift away from traditional methods that often prioritize geographic regions or specific threats. This approach relies on integrating data from diverse sources to build a comprehensive understanding of each object. By understanding object profiles and relationships, analysts can use this form of intelligence to predict future events and potential threats. Changes in object attributes, relationships, or activities can serve as early warnings.1 Object-Based Production. OBP is the standardized IC process that organizes data around objects of interest, enabling users to aggregate information across domains and tie it to a specific object. The USAF intelligence enterprise translates this OBP methodology into a mission line known as the disposition of forces (DOF) production process, which supplements theater order of battle and is central to enabling the execution of theater dynamic targets at speed and scale.

chunk_text: The USAF intelligence enterprise translates this OBP methodology into a mission line known as the disposition of forces (DOF) production process, which supplements theater order of battle and is central to enabling the execution of theater dynamic targets at speed and scale. While both OBI and OBP leverage the concept of "objects," they operate differently and serve distinct purposes. The connection is that they both use a consistent object-based data model.2 Terms associated with OBP include: Object. An object is a digital representation of a real-world thing, action, or abstraction. An object is not always a target. Once an object is vetted and validated through the target development process, it may then be designated as a target. In the dynamic targeting process, objects become tracks, and tracks become targets. Object Management System (OMS). OMS is the application, service, or system-of-systems that enables the production and consumption of information and intelligence within an object-based data construct, aligning all reporting to objects agnostic to the producer or consumer. Structured Observation Management (SOM).

chunk_text: Structured Observation Management (SOM). SOM is the method by which geospatial intelligence (GEOINT) observations from satellites, sensors, and other sources are captured, structured, stored, and shared in a standardized format compatible with the OBP. This ensures that GEOINT information can be integrated into the IC’s OBP process. Disposition of Forces (DOF). DOF is the identity, location, activity, and operational status of an object or forces. DOF is informed by the relationships between objects, their assigned garrison locations, and associations to other units, however, the execution of DOF does not cease when objects enter or depart from garrison. DOF provides planners the ability to perform quick queries to provide a dynamic picture of where the adversaries were in the past, where they are going, and where they will be soon.

chunk_text: Throughout the campaign, it continuously assesses the effectiveness of operations, measuring progress, revealing emerging opportunities, and guiding necessary adjustments to maintain initiative in a dynamic environment. Intelligence is defined as a product resulting from the collection, processing, integration, evaluation, analysis, and interpretation of available information concerning foreign nations, hostile or potentially hostile forces or elements, or areas of actual or potential operations. It is also the activities that result in the product as well as the organizations that conduct them. Intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) are integrated capabilities that collect, process, exploit, and disseminate accurate, timely information to provide the situational awareness necessary to plan and conduct operations. The goal of ISR is to provide precise, timely intelligence to the warfighters and to help the USAF achieve information superiority. ISR activities are how raw data and information are gathered from the OE to answer a specific intelligence requirement. Intelligence and ISR form a connected but distinct value chain.

chunk_text: Intelligence and ISR form a connected but distinct value chain. ISR provides the foundational input: platforms, sensors, and personnel dedicated to collecting raw data from the OE. In its unprocessed state, this data has limited utility for commanders. The intelligence enterprise performs the essential value-added function, transforming that data through exploitation, analysis, and contextualization. The result is intelligence, a refined product that fosters understanding, reduces uncertainty, and enables effective decision-making. This distinction is operationally significant. Resourcing collection without a proportional investment in analytic capacity produces diminishing returns and fails to generate the decision superiority required to compete and win.

The aim of battlespace characterization is to identify what is and is not known by continuously updating and validating assessments. Information gained through battlespace characterization is a valuable foundation to inform targeting.

Text: Collection operations acquire raw data and information about relevant aspects of the OE and provide that information to intelligence processing and exploitation elements. Collection involves tasking and synchronizing ISR sensors, platforms, and exploitation resources to characterize the OE, adversary activities, and infrastructure, and to find, fix, track entities of interest. chunk_text: Intelligence is integral to every military operation, but its application is never one-size-fits-all. chunk_text: In support of combat operations, individual unit-level intelligence teams are collectively organized to form the contingency intelligence network (CIN).4 Wing intelligence personnel form the main teams of the CIN; the combat intelligence cell (CIC), mission planning cell (MPC), and squadron intelligence. The CIN funnels intelligence into and out of a particular wing or organization to support three main functions: mission planning, mission execution, and mission debriefing and reporting. At the operational level, the CIC interacts with the unit support function inside the AOC’s ISR division (ISRD) to gather current intelligence, threat data, and request for information (RFI) responses. They disseminate this information throughout the wing and down to operational squadrons. The MPC provides analysis of possible enemy COA, support to targeting, and timely updates to operational mission planners. Squadron intelligence personnel support specific airframes and communicate relevant intelligence directly to aircrew. The CIN, through the CIC, MPC, and squadron intelligence, keeps the commander and operations crews informed of intelligence that can affect mission execution. The CIN, through the CIC, MPC, and squadron intelligence, keeps the commander and operations crews informed of intelligence that can affect mission execution. Support may include intelligence databases, current threat briefings and training, and assistance with mission planning.

chunk_text: USAF special operations are intelligence-intensive and, in some cases, require detailed and tailored intelligence support. Special operations forces (SOF) support tends to rely on organic ISR assets, be less centralized, and focus on tactical intelligence. Intelligence personnel produce detailed, specialized products tailored to the mission, customer, and pace of operation. The sensitivity of SOF missions may constrain the release of post-mission reports. Commanders should report sensitive mission data through special access or routine intelligence channels accordingly. 4 See Air Force Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (AFTTP) 3-4.14, Contingency Intelligence Network, for additional information.

chunk_text: When lawful and authorized to do so, USAF intelligence forces may be tasked to conduct ISR or incident awareness and assessment (IAA) within the homeland. Though similar to ISR, IAA operations are conducted during defense support of civil authorities (DSCA) activities, providing timely and usable information to commanders and local, tribal, state, and federal leaders to save lives, reduce human suffering, and protect property. Though ISR activities may be conducted within the US for homeland defense, IAA is only conducted within the US for DSCA. Importantly, the laws governing DoW operations in the homeland are different than those that govern overseas operations. These laws place specific restrictions and limitations on the employment or use of military intelligence in the homeland.5

chunk_text: Cyberspace operations that support air operations are usually provided to the air component through reachback and are broken down into three subsets: offensive (OCO), defensive (DCO), and Department of Defense Information Network (DoDIN) operations. Intelligence support to each of these subsets is technologically focused and requires significant technical understanding of the domain. Cyberspace intelligence analysts integrate as closely as possible with cyberspace personnel. Intelligence for OCO identifies access vectors to gain entry into adversarial target networks, detail target network characteristics, and identify network vulnerabilities for further attack vectors. Intelligence analysts supporting DCO and DoDIN operations consider the perspective of potential adversaries that may attack air component networks to understand threats to those systems, whether from state or non-state actors.7

chunk_text: Long-range kill chains are becoming essential for future conflicts because adversaries are investing in anti-access and area-denial systems, longrange fires, and distributed operations that demand we sense, decide, and act from far beyond traditional engagement distances. As threats grow more mobile, more concealed, and more geographically dispersed, the ability to execute a seamless chain of sensing, sense-making, and engagement across domains becomes the only way to maintain decision advantage. Future fights will unfold at machine speed, over vast distances, and under constant threat of disruption, which means forces should be able to detect targets early, validate them with confidence, and pair them to the right shooter without delay. As the force moves toward more contested, multi domain environments, the integration of advanced ISR, resilient C2, and precision fires makes long-range kill chains not just useful, but foundational to deterrence and combat credibility. Long-range kill chains function when the strategic, operational, and tactical levels of intelligence operate as a single, synchronized enterprise.

chunk_text: CHAPTER 2: COMMAND AND CONTROL, FORCE PRESENTATION, AND ORGANIZATION Air component intelligence forces provide the foundation for a commander’s ability to understand, anticipate, and shape the OE. They generate decision-quality intelligence, integrate seamlessly with joint and IC partners, and execute a spectrum of activities that include indications and warning, targeting, characterizing the threat environment, performing collection, and assessment. These forces operate through a mix of assigned and attached elements, with authorities and support responsibilities shifting based on command relationships. Their effectiveness is amplified through robust support networks, federated production centers, national level agencies, and other reachback organizations. These extend analytic capacity and ensure the air component maintains a persistent, comprehensive understanding of the adversary.

chunk_text: A combatant commander (CCDR) employs assigned and attached intelligence forces to achieve national and theater objectives. When additional forces are required, CCDRs submit a request for forces (RFF) through the global force management (GFM) process. When authorized, CCMDs may also coordinate to enable the cooperative use of assets not regularly assigned to them to improve coverage. Based on guidance and direction from the CCDR, intelligence (J2) and operations (J3) staffs develop an overall theater collection strategy and posture to execute intelligence operations. The CCDR establishes priorities for intelligence operations that align with national and theater objectives and ensures theater planning efforts support the fulfillment of crisis intelligence requirements. High-priority, time-sensitive requirements are identified and pre-validated by the JFC for the air component commander to consider for dynamic retasking during the execution of intelligence operations. When theater requirements exceed the capacity of assigned and attached forces, the JFC or air component commander may submit requests for additional ISR capabilities (personnel, platforms) through the CCMD.

chunk_text: When theater requirements exceed the capacity of assigned and attached forces, the JFC or air component commander may submit requests for additional ISR capabilities (personnel, platforms) through the CCMD. Taskings involving ISR assets supporting more than one JFC are coordinated and deconflicted by a common superior commander. The CCDR may delegate operational control (OPCON) or tactical control (TACON) over theater ISR assets to appropriate subordinate commanders. However, the CCDR retains the authority to validate and prioritize requirements for collection by theater ISR assets. At the theater level, CCDRs exercise collection management authority (CMA) for collection operations within their area of responsibility (AOR).9 Collection Management Authority (CMA). CMA is the authority to establish, prioritize, and validate theater collection requirements, establish sensor tasking guidance, and develop theater-wide collection policies. Commanders exercising OPCON over intelligence forces may assume CMA of tasked ISR assets as part of the delegation of authority.

chunk_text: Commanders exercising OPCON over intelligence forces may assume CMA of tasked ISR assets as part of the delegation of authority. The theater J2 usually retains CMA to validate, modify, or non-concur theater intelligence requirements through the CCDR’s joint intelligence operations center (JIOC). CMA usually includes the authority to task geospatial-intelligence sensors and lower-echelon signals intelligence (SIGINT) collection systems with more localized collection capabilities. The National Security Agency (NSA) retains CMA over strategic-capable SIGINT ISR systems. In addition, CCDRs may request, and subsequently receive, temporary SIGINT operational tasking authority (SOTA) over theater-wide capable platforms and sensors. The delegation of SOTA to a CCDR and subsequent delegation of this authority to the JFC ensures the theater can prioritize requirements and focus SIGINT collection where needed to carry out assigned missions. Collection management includes two complementary functions: collection requirements management (CRM) and collection operations management (COM). • Collection Requirements Management (CRM).

chunk_text: Collection management includes two complementary functions: collection requirements management (CRM) and collection operations management (COM). • Collection Requirements Management (CRM). CRM is the authoritative development and control of collection, processing, exploitation, and information reporting requirements. CRM defines what intelligence systems should collect. • Collection Operations Management (COM). COM is the authoritative direction, scheduling, and control of specific collection operations and associated processing, exploitation, and information reporting resources. The COM process specifies how to satisfy the requirement and provides details on how the collection occurs. The CCDR’s staff is responsible for developing a collection strategy and execution posture for ISR missions and coordinating with national agencies. The theater J2 reviews, validates, and prioritizes all outstanding intelligence requirements, whether originating with the J2 staff or subordinate components.

chunk_text: The theater J2 reviews, validates, and prioritizes all outstanding intelligence requirements, whether originating with the J2 staff or subordinate components. In conjunction with component staffs, the JFC's staff is responsible for developing a shared production architecture that leverages reachback, distributed, and federated partners for intelligence exploitation and analysis. This shared production architecture also leverages support from organic capabilities at the component and JTF levels, the Services, other CCDRs, foreign partners, and national agencies. This helps maximize coverage of intelligence requirements. Control of Assets vs Control of Intelligence. A critical distinction for commanders at all echelons is the separation of authority between controlling an ISR asset and control of the intelligence it gathers. The CCDR’s delegation of OPCON and TACON over an ISR 9 For additional information on collection management, see JP 2-0, Joint Intelligence. asset provides the subordinate commander with authority over the platform’s employment, but it does not automatically grant authority over the raw intelligence it collects. SIGINT serves as a primary example.

chunk_text: SIGINT serves as a primary example. Regardless of the air component platform collecting the data (e.g., MQ-9, RQ-4, RC-135V/W), the raw SIGINT is owned by and routed through the NSA for processing, exploitation, and dissemination (PED). With any command relationship involving intelligence forces, care should be taken to know, understand, and align with the appropriate authorities. Joint Intelligence Operations Center (JIOC). Each CCMD and some subordinate unified commands have assigned JIOCs to integrate intelligence capabilities in support of their mission. The JIOC supports joint planning and conducts intelligence operations in support of the CCDR and staff, subordinate component commands, and JTFs. The JIOC integrates intelligence from external DoW and national intelligence organizations, Partner Nations, and law enforcement to ensure accurate, timely, and complete intelligence is available to support CCMD joint planning, execution, and assessment.

section: ASSIGNED AND ATTACHED AIR COMPONENT INTELLIGENCE FORCES
chunk_text: Air Force Intelligence Forces. USAF intelligence forces are normally presented to JFCs through an AOC or air expeditionary task force (AETF) structure. USAF intelligence forces are sometimes employed in support of other components through joint expeditionary taskings. These taskings are Service-specific and allocated based on requests for forces submitted by a CCDR. Similarly, the USAF conducts many peacetime intelligence operations in support of CCMDs or interagency customers that do not support a JTF and do not fall under an AETF construct. When Airmen are tasked to augment another Service, the AETF model should be applied as a template. Most USAF ISR assets are considered high-demand, low-density (HD/LD) and are often tasked to perform a range of missions across the globe. The Joint Staff coordinates with the military departments/services, CCDRs, and intelligence agencies to identify and recommend joint global platform- and sensor-based ISR along with associated PED capability sourcing solutions. Some assets may be theater assigned.

chunk_text: Some assets may be theater assigned. When emergent requirements exceed the capabilities or capacity of assigned forces, SecWar can allocate additional forces to CCMDs through the Global Force Management Allocation Plan (GFMAP). For both assigned and allocated forces, SecWar specifies the command relationship authorities. Intelligence, Surveillance, Reconnaissance Division (ISRD). The ISRD, in conjunction with the other AOC divisions, plans and executes airborne ISR operations and provides combat ISR support to air component planning, execution, and assessment activities. The ISRD serves as the central node and fusion center for all air component intelligence operations. Its primary mission is to provide the air component commander an understanding of the adversary and the OE. The ISRD is fundamentally responsible for orienting the commander to enemy capabilities, threats, and COG by directing and managing the air components intelligence operations. It accomplishes this by seamlessly integrating intelligence functions, including analysis, collection management, and targeting support within the air tasking cycle.

chunk_text: USAF intelligence at the wing level through their subordinate squadrons provides a comprehensive spectrum of support, delivering critical analysis and production that spans every level of warfare. At the strategic level, service intelligence centers produce foundational intelligence on adversary capabilities and future threats, informing national policy, long-term defense planning, and weapon system acquisition. This strategic foundation enables the operational efforts of other specialized intelligence wings, which provide direct support to air component and joint force commanders through missions like real-time ISR analysis, precision targeting, and cryptologic operations. This support is further refined at the tactical level, where intelligence personnel organic to flying wings and squadrons provide direct mission planning, threat analysis, and debriefing for aircrews. This unit-level function ensures warfighters have an immediate, detailed understanding of the environment and adversary actions relevant to their specific sorties.

chunk_text: This unit-level function ensures warfighters have an immediate, detailed understanding of the environment and adversary actions relevant to their specific sorties. Many of the intelligence wings function as service-retained forces, meaning they are managed and allocated by the USAF to support joint force requirements. Through the GFM process, ACC works with component commanders to align intelligence manpower against their most pressing analytical and targeting needs. This support is formalized through specific tasking orders, such as the Analytic Support Tasking Request Order (ASTRO) for analytical efforts, or the Targeting Support Tasking Request Order (TSTRO) for targeting needs. This model allows the USAF to retain stewardship of its specialized intelligence capabilities while ensuring they remain responsive to the needs of the warfighter. 15 For additional information on Air Force Intelligence policy, see Department of the Air Force Manual (DAFMAN) 14-401, Intelligence Analysis And Targeting Tradecraft/Data Standards.

chunk_text: The joint intelligence process provides the basis for common intelligence terminology and procedures. It consists of six interrelated categories of intelligence operations and serves as a comprehensive framework to guide intelligence activities of the joint services and broader IC. This joint process feeds analytical efforts via the joint intelligence preparation of the operational environment (JIPOE), a four-step analytical process used by joint intelligence organizations to produce estimates and other intelligence products in support of the JFC’s decision-making process. In contrast to JIPOE, Services use the intelligence preparation of the battlespace (IPB). IPB mirrors the four-step process of JIPOE, but differs in the purpose, focus, and level of detail. Service component intelligence personnel provide force specific, detailed analysis via IPB that supports component operations and complements the broader JIPOE. The USAF intelligence enterprise contributes to every step of the JIPOE, IPB, and the joint intelligence process. The USAF is the only service to refer to the joint intelligence process as an acronym (PCPADE).16 Planning and Direction.

chunk_text: The USAF is the only service to refer to the joint intelligence process as an acronym (PCPADE).16 Planning and Direction. Planning and direction set the foundation for the entire intelligence process. Leaders identify what they need to know, establish priorities, and issue guidance that shapes all subsequent intelligence activities. This phase ensures that limited resources are focused on the most important questions, aligning intelligence efforts with operational goals and strategic objectives. Collection. Collection involves gathering raw information from a wide range of sources: human, signals, imagery, open source, and more. The goal is to acquire relevant data that can answer the requirements established during planning. Effective collection balances speed, accuracy, and risk while ensuring coverage across all required domains. Processing and Exploitation. Once information is collected, it must be converted into usable form. Processing and exploitation include translating, decrypting, organizing, and filtering raw data so analysts can work with it.

This phase ensures intelligence reaches the right people at the right time and is incorporated into planning and operations.

The subset of these requirements related to the adversary and the OE are known as priority intelligence requirements (PIRs), and they serve as the primary drivers for all intelligence activities. When an intelligence question cannot be answered with organic assets or available information, the formal RFI process is initiated, tasking the broader IC to leverage its vast resources. chunk_text: Joint Planning Process for Air (JPPA). JPPA is driven by clearly defined information requirements, and intelligence is the element that gives those requirements meaning. JPPA translates the JFC’s strategic aims into executable operational airpower, and intelligence underpins every decision within that process. Intelligence professionals are embedded throughout, ensuring commanders and planners understand the adversary, the environment, and the opportunities and risks inherent in each COA.17 Intelligence analysts enter planning with a detailed understanding of the adversary’s capabilities, vulnerabilities, order of battle, decision-making processes, and likely/most dangerous COA. This understanding shapes the commander’s operational approach and informs the development of feasible, acceptable, and effective airpower options. All source analysts develop the adversary picture, assess intent, identify critical vulnerabilities, and anticipate adversary reactions across the range of potential friendly actions.

chunk_text: All source analysts develop the adversary picture, assess intent, identify critical vulnerabilities, and anticipate adversary reactions across the range of potential friendly actions. Collection strategists integrate sensor capabilities into planning by recommending collection allocations, conducting feasibility assessments, estimating ISR gain-loss, and ensuring alignment with component requirements and available capabilities. Targeting strategists guide the development of the operational targeting strategy, ensuring target systems are understood, desired effects are achievable, and analyst teams can match the right targets to the commanders’ objectives. Together, these intelligence roles ensure that JPPA is grounded in a realistic understanding of battlespace and that airpower options are aligned with both strategic aims and operational realities. Normally developed by the AOC Strategy Division (SRD) or air component A5, the joint air operations plan (JAOP) is the comprehensive plan that translates the JFC and JFACC guidance into a coherent strategy of the air, and cyberspace operations.

chunk_text: Normally developed by the AOC Strategy Division (SRD) or air component A5, the joint air operations plan (JAOP) is the comprehensive plan that translates the JFC and JFACC guidance into a coherent strategy of the air, and cyberspace operations. The ISR operations strategy is a key component of the JAOP, ensuring that collection and analysis are synchronized with theater and national efforts to support the overall campaign.18 The AFFOR A2 staff provides intelligence inputs to the operations order (OPORD), which identify forces, functional support, and resources and their flow into theater in support of the mission. Both documents include Annex B, developed by ISR strategists and planners, which details how intelligence will support the larger air plan, and identifies critical obstacles and shortfalls. Both plans follow the standard “SMEAC” format. These planning efforts culminate in operational directives and strategies such as the Air 17 For additional information on JPPA, see AFDP 5-0 Planning. 18 For additional information about the JAOP, see JP 3-30, Joint Air Operations, and AFDP 5-0, Planning. Operations Directive (AOD), ISR Operations Strategy, and ISR Allocation Plan.

chunk_text: Operations Directive (AOD), ISR Operations Strategy, and ISR Allocation Plan. Air Operations Directive (AOD). The AOD is a near-term directive that translates the broader JAOP into daily operational guidance for the air tasking order (ATO). It refines and prioritizes operational tasks, including specific guidance for ISR operations. The AOD details the JFACC’s intent for a specific ATO or time period, lists PIRs, specifies employment of certain ISR platforms and intelligence disciplines (INTs), and provide PED guidance.19 This guidance is crucial for the daily development of the ISR plan, including the component prioritized collection list (CPCL) and the final reconnaissance, surveillance, and target acquisition (RSTA) Annex. ISR Operations Strategy. The air component ISR strategy is driven by overarching J2 guidance and priorities, and commander PIRs help focus this effort. ISR strategists integrate a wide range of capabilities into the plan: national, theater, and other service platforms, and understand the PED architecture.

chunk_text: ISR strategists integrate a wide range of capabilities into the plan: national, theater, and other service platforms, and understand the PED architecture. Strategists also work with AFFOR staff planners to identify platform, sensor, PED, communications availability, force flow, timing, bed down, and sustainment matters. Other components’ tasks have implications for the air component’s ISR capabilities, so ISR shortfalls and limitations with broad implications for the joint force should be communicated clearly and early in venues such as the joint collection working group (JCWG) or joint collection management board (JCMB). ISR Apportionment and Allocation Plan. Air apportionment is the determination and assignment of the total expected effort by percentage and/or by priority that should be devoted to various air operations for a given period of time. The JFACC apportions air to the CCDR or JTF, who serves as the approval authority. Air allocation is the translation of the air apportionment decision into the total number of sorties or missions by weapons system type available for each operation or task.

chunk_text: Collection operations acquire raw data about the OE and provide it to intelligence processing elements. This involves the detailed tasking and synchronization of ISR sensor platforms and exploitation resources to characterize the OE, adversary activities, and infrastructure, and to support the targeting cycle. The overarching purpose of collection operations is typically driven by the need for battlespace characterization, targeting, test assumptions, confirm knowledge, identify intelligence gaps, and enhance the commander’s decision advantage. This is done through collection management, which is the process of converting intelligence requirements into collection requirements (CRs), establishing priorities, tasking or coordinating with appropriate collection sources or agencies, monitoring results, and retasking, as required. Collection operations orchestrate three distinct but related functions: collection management authority (CMA), collection requirements management (CRM), and collections operations management (COM).

This is done through collection management, which is the process of converting intelligence requirements into collection requirements (CRs), establishing priorities, tasking or coordinating with appropriate collection sources or agencies, monitoring results, and retasking, as required. Collection operations orchestrate three distinct but related functions: collection management authority (CMA), collection requirements management (CRM), and collections operations management (COM).

chunk_text: Joint Integrated Prioritized Collection List (JIPCL). The JIPCL is a comprehensive, prioritized list of all CPCL CR that guides the tasking of both theater and national ISR assets. Managed by the ISRD, the RSTA synchronizes and directs the employment of all available ISR assets from national systems to theater and tactical platforms to collect information that answers the commanders’ PIRs and fills critical intelligence gaps. The data gathered through RSTA missions feeds PED activities, where it is transformed into actionable intelligence.

chunk_text: Airborne Systems. Airborne ISR platforms provide commanders with a taskable and often visible presence, capable of collecting and disseminating large volumes of real-time information across a wide area. To mitigate threats or political sensitivities, these platforms may operate in a standoff capacity, which can limit the range and depth of sensor coverage. A critical consideration for all airborne ISR is the significant PED support required to turn collected data into usable intelligence, which can be 20 For additional information on JIPCL, see JP 2-0 Joint Intelligence. accomplished via reachback to specialized ground stations. When dedicated ISR assets are insufficient, planners should integrate non-traditional ISR (NTISR) platforms, such as strike aircraft, to supplement collection. This tactic, sometimes called operations reconnaissance (ops recce), leverages the advanced sensors on multi-role aircraft to increase battlespace awareness. The successful use of NTISR requires clear, pre-defined mission authorities that distinguish between aircraft control, which remains with the pilot-in-command, and the separate authorities for sensor control and sensor tasking.

chunk_text: The successful use of NTISR requires clear, pre-defined mission authorities that distinguish between aircraft control, which remains with the pilot-in-command, and the separate authorities for sensor control and sensor tasking. This ensures that even when an aircraft is performing multiple roles, the C2 of its intelligence collection function is unambiguous and effectively integrated into the broader ISR plan. Space-based Systems. Space-based intelligence systems are integral to modern military operations, offering the primary advantage of persistent, wide-area coverage over denied areas where airborne or ground assets cannot operate. Their longevity and reduced vulnerability provide commanders with critical information to assess situations and develop plans. However, these systems are a limited resource, as demand often exceeds capacity, and their orbital mechanics can create coverage gaps. Furthermore, they can be susceptible to adversary denial and deception techniques, necessitating a clear understanding of their capabilities and limitations during planning. The US employs a variety of military, national, and non-military space systems to support its objectives.

chunk_text: The US employs a variety of military, national, and non-military space systems to support its objectives. Military and national satellite systems, controlled by the DoW and IC respectively, provide a broad range of capabilities from environmental monitoring to detailed imagery intelligence. Access to these high-demand assets requires detailed justification and must be coordinated well in advance through the appropriate command channels. To augment these limited resources, commanders can also leverage non-military systems, including allied or commercial assets, though access often requires pre-negotiated legal arrangements that should be established prior to the start of operations. Ground-based Systems. Ground-based intelligence systems are critical components of the collection architecture, tasked worldwide to gather information for disciplines such as SIGINT and Measurement and Signature Intelligence (MASINT), fulfilling national, theater, and local requirements. These systems include air surveillance and target acquisition radars, which, while primarily for air control, also provide valuable threat warning within designated areas.

chunk_text: These systems include air surveillance and target acquisition radars, which, while primarily for air control, also provide valuable threat warning within designated areas. However, these radar systems are limited by atmospheric conditions, electronic jamming, and line-of-sight constraints. Beyond tactical air surveillance, significant ground-based ISR resources are dedicated to missile warning and space surveillance. Ground-based missile warning sensors primarily identify and characterize ballistic missile attacks, while also contributing to space surveillance. The Space Surveillance Network (SSN) actively tracks and characterizes artificial objects in space, providing crucial data on adversary space order of battle, overflight warnings, and spacecraft status. This information offers theater commanders early warning and supports denial and deception efforts against potential threats. Cyberspace-based Systems. Cyberspace-based ISR capabilities are integral to military forces and enable operations across the competition continuum. Cyberspace-focused ISR includes digital network analysis and intelligence support to USAF cyberspace missions.

chunk_text: GEOINT is the exploitation and analysis of imagery and geospatial information to describe, assess, and visually depict physical features and geographically referenced activities on or about the Earth. GEOINT consists of imagery, IMINT, and geospatial information. GEOINT data sources include commercial satellites, government satellites, aircraft, maps, commercial databases, census information, global positioning system waypoints, and utility schematics. GEOINT can synthesize intelligence and data into conceptualized geographic spatial content that can provide commanders key operational intelligence (e.g., best vantage point for shooters, most advantageous entry points, and spatial trends and patterns). The National Geospatial Intelligence Agency (NGA) serves as the IC and DoW functional manager for GEOINT. IMINT is the technical, geographic, and intelligence information derived through interpreting or analyzing imagery and collateral materials. IMINT includes the exploitation of imagery data derived from electro-optical, radar, infrared, multi-spectral, and laser sensors.

GEOINT consists of imagery, IMINT, and geospatial information. GEOINT data sources include commercial satellites, government satellites, aircraft, maps, commercial databases, census information, global positioning system waypoints, and utility schematics. GEOINT can synthesize intelligence and data into conceptualized geographic spatial content that can provide commanders key operational intelligence (e.g., best vantage point for shooters, most advantageous entry points, and spatial trends and patterns). The National Geospatial Intelligence Agency (NGA) serves as the IC and DoW functional manager for GEOINT. IMINT is the technical, geographic, and intelligence information derived through interpreting or analyzing imagery and collateral materials. IMINT includes the exploitation of imagery data derived from electro-optical, radar, infrared, multi-spectral, and laser sensors.

IMINT results from the processing and exploitation of raw imagery (information) to create an analyzed product (intelligence). IMINT is used for historical comparisons, to locate adversary military forces and facilities, and to provide insight into the adversary’s capabilities. chunk_text: HUMINT is an intelligence collection discipline that uses people in an area of interest to identify or provide insight into adversary plans and intentions, research and development, strategy, doctrine, and capabilities. Dedicated HUMINT collectors amplify, clarify, or verify information collected by other airborne, ground-based, or space-based assets. In many cases, HUMINT may be the best source regarding adversary intentions. The USAF has an organic HUMINT capability and works with DoW and other national-level agencies to collect on priority USAF operational and strategic requirements. The Director of the CIA serves as the National HUMINT Manager, and the Director of the DIA serves as the DoW functional manager.

chunk_text: SIGINT is a category of intelligence comprised of COMINT, ELINT, and FISINT, that may consist of an individual element, or a combination. SIGINT uses intercepted electromagnetic emissions to provide information on adversary forces' capabilities, intentions, formations, and locations. SIGINT also includes collecting, processing, and exploiting data from information in cyberspace. The air component’s link to the SIGINT enterprise is the National Tactical Integration (NTI) specialty team in the ISRD. The NSA serves as the IC functional manager for SIGINT.23 Communications Intelligence (COMINT). COMINT consists of information derived from intercepting and monitoring the adversary's communications systems. COMINT exploits an adversary’s communications, revealing capabilities, intentions, perceived vulnerabilities, and, often, an adversary’s perception of the US and multinational partners. Electronic Intelligence (ELINT). ELINT is intelligence derived from interception and analysis of non-communications emitters, such as radars. ELINT includes both operational electronic intelligence (OPELINT) and technical electronic intelligence (TECHELINT).

chunk_text: ELINT includes both operational electronic intelligence (OPELINT) and technical electronic intelligence (TECHELINT). OPELINT focuses on operationally relevant information (location, movement, employment, tactics, and activity of foreign non-communications emitters and associated weapons systems). TECHELINT concentrates on the technical aspects (signal characteristics, modes, functions, associations, capabilities, limitations, vulnerabilities, and technology levels). Foreign Instrumentation Signature Intelligence (FISINT). FISINT consists of technical information derived by intercepting electromagnetic emissions associated with testing and operational deployment of foreign air and space, surface, and subsurface systems. These emissions are typically generated by telemetry, electronic interrogators, and video data links. The technical details of foreign weapons system development derived from FISINT can provide insight into foreign capabilities, identify avenues for exploitation, and/or aid the employment of appropriate countermeasures. FISINT is accomplished by specialized, national-level Service and DoW organizations. chunk_text: MASINT is technically derived intelligence to detect, locate, and describe the physical measurements and signatures intrinsic to targets and events. MASINT collects and produces precise threat characteristics and performance information essential to support planning, development, and application of weapon systems, countermeasures, targeting, and battle damage assessment. MASINT is especially useful to monitor adversary technical developments and deployments, as well as emerging threats from weapons of mass destruction. DIA leads the IC effort for MASINT via senior science and technology officers (SSTOs) who integrate DIA efforts to meet operational intelligence requirements.

chunk_text: OSINT is intelligence derived from publicly available information (PAI) that is collected, exploited, and disseminated in a timely manner to an appropriate audience for the purpose of addressing a specific intelligence requirement. PAI is information that has been published or broadcast for public consumption, available on request to the public, accessible online, could be seen or heard by a casual observer, or obtained by visiting a place or event open to the public. OSINT complements other intelligence disciplines and may cue collection and analysis, fill intelligence gaps, and supplement the accuracy and fidelity of classified information databases. Importantly, OSINT is susceptible to manipulation and deception and requires research expertise and OPSEC for internetbased activities. The Director of the CIA is the IC functional manager for OSINT, and the DIA Director is the DoW’s functional manager.

Each category is aligned to a distinct purpose and decision need. All-source intelligence analysis is inherently complex, supporting national, theater, and tactical consumers while simultaneously sustaining multiple production lines, including foundational, time-sensitive reporting, and tactical mission analysis. USAF intelligence professionals integrate contributions from all eighteen IC organizations, 24 For additional information on intelligence disciplines, see JP 2-0, Joint Intelligence. fusing national-level products with time-dominant reporting to generate decision-quality intelligence for the air component and the joint force.25 General Military Intelligence (GMI). GMI is intelligence concerning foreign countries' military capabilities or topics affecting potential US or multinational military operations. Current intelligence and GMI form a symbiotic relationship.

chunk_text: Current intelligence and GMI form a symbiotic relationship. Information gained during the development of current intelligence forms the basis for the GMI effort and other analytical products. Conversely, GMI provides underlying threat information to produce accurate and meaningful current intelligence. GMI focuses on a wide range of factors, including order of battle, organization, DOF, training, tactics, and overall strategy to determine a nation's military strength and effectiveness. GMI also analyzes critical supporting elements such as transportation networks, military materiel production, military and civilian communications systems, and military economics, including any foreign military assistance. Furthermore, it considers the potential use of asymmetric tactics or terror attacks and involves the detailed location, identification, and description of military-related installations.

chunk_text: It covers foreign developments in basic applied research and applied engineering techniques. S&TI also covers scientific and technical characteristics, capabilities, and limitations of foreign military systems. This analysis includes weapons, weapon systems, and materiel, the related research and development, and the methods used to manufacture the foreign military system. Example S&TI products include weapon system characteristics, capabilities, vulnerabilities, limitations, effectiveness, research and development, and related manufacturing information. S&TI products play a vital role in the acquisition process by allowing the procurement of systems or systems upgrades to meet current, developing, and future threats. Acquisition Intelligence. Acquisition intelligence addresses future threats, specializing in S&TI to anticipate enemy capabilities in advance by one to five years. Acquisitions intelligence brings a focus on supportability issues associated with new ISR capabilities planned to be brought into theater. It is especially important for multinational operations. chunk_text: It is especially important for multinational operations. Identity Intelligence. Identity intelligence results from the fusion of identity attributes (biologic, biographic, behavioral, and reputational information related to individuals), and other information and intelligence associated with those attributes, collected across various intelligence disciplines. The goal of identity intelligence is to discover unknown potential threat actors by connecting individuals to other persons, places, events, or materials, analyzing patterns of life, and characterizing the threat posed to US interests. Target Intelligence. Intelligence operations play a prominent role in the targeting cycle by detecting, locating, and identifying targets and supporting mission planning and assessment. Target intelligence portrays and locates the components of a target or target complex and indicates its vulnerability and relative importance. Required products, such as target imagery, should be immediately available to support the air tasking cycle. ISR assets may be employed to detect and identify potential targets, changes to existing targets, or to conduct battle damage assessment.

chunk_text: ISR assets may be employed to detect and identify potential targets, changes to existing targets, or to conduct battle damage assessment. Multiple missions may be required to provide the level of detail necessary to support the precision engagement of specific targets. Target intelligence products include annotated target graphics, electronic target folders, target system analyses, and geospatial information to provide comprehensive data needed to plan and generate lethal and nonlethal effects. Targeting within the air tasking cycle operates inside a compressed 72-hr window. Here, the focus shifts from foundational target development to the selection and prioritization of already developed targets from the restricted target list (RTL) or joint target list (JTL) for inclusion on the component target list (CTL). Intelligence analysts and targeteers attend joint targeting working groups (JTWG) and joint targeting coordination boards (JTCB) to approve targets for the JIPTL to meet commander’s objectives.

Regardless of the cycle, intelligence personnel constantly evaluate the adversary and update target information, ensuring that targeting, whether deliberate or dynamic, is based on the most current and accurate intelligence available.26 Current Intelligence. Current intelligence is produced by fusing data and intelligence products regarding the current situation in a particular area or the activities of specific groups. This type of intelligence is similar to warning intelligence in that both require continuous monitoring of world events and specific activities in an operational area. Information required to produce current intelligence products includes continuous assessments of adversary intentions, capabilities, and the will to use military force, as well as the identification of potential centers of gravity, operational plans, and vulnerabilities.

chunk_text: Information required to produce current intelligence products includes continuous assessments of adversary intentions, capabilities, and the will to use military force, as well as the identification of potential centers of gravity, operational plans, and vulnerabilities. It also encompasses geographic, environmental, and social analysis of the operational area, monitoring significant military and political events, and the status of strategic transportation nodes such as major airfields and seaports. A critical component of current intelligence is maintaining the adversary's DOF, a product of the DCGS enterprise that provides the precise geographic location and status of known units, directly enabling the Find, Fix, Track (F2T) phase of the dynamic targeting process. Estimative Intelligence. Estimative intelligence provides forecasts of current or potential situations that may have bearing on the planning and execution of military operations, otherwise known as estimates. By employing pattern analysis, inference, and statistical probability techniques, intelligence personnel produce estimates describing relevant actors’ capabilities and behavior. Warning Intelligence.

chunk_text: Warning Intelligence. Warning intelligence products are derived from a worldwide system that analyzes and integrates information to assess the probability of hostile actions and provide sufficient warning to preempt or counter their effects. Warning intelligence systems rely on information and indications from sources at all levels. The focus of warning intelligence products varies at each echelon and is most specific at the operational and tactical levels. Warning intelligence products are specifically designed to alert decision-makers to emerging threats and potential crises, focusing on adversary intentions, capabilities, past behaviors, and doctrine. This includes monitoring foreign government responses to crises, analyzing significant political, economic, or social situations in both friendly and adversary states that could trigger instability, and tracking changes in adversary force dispositions, military activities, and mobilization status.

chunk_text: USAF intelligence personnel adhere to analytical tradecraft standards common across the entire joint force and the IC to ensure rigor and clarity in their assessments. A fundamental aspect of this is the formal expression of confidence in analytical judgments. Confidence is the value placed on an analysis, “high confidence” in target identification, for example, increases combat effectiveness while reducing risks such as fratricide and collateral damage. Conversely, “low confidence” indicates a greater risk to the mission and friendly forces due to an incomplete understanding of the environment. These levels are not a guess, they are determined by systematically evaluating three factors: the number of key assumptions, the credibility and diversity of sources used, and the overall strength of the logical argument. Analysts then assign a confidence level of low, moderate, or high to their judgments, providing commanders with a clear and standardized measure of the certainty behind the intelligence they receive for decision-making.27

chunk_text: The dissemination and integration step involves the delivery to and use of intelligence. Dissemination ensures commanders, planners, and operational forces receive finished intelligence in time to make effective decisions and execute operations. The USAF’s distributed operations capability provides timely and tailored intelligence across the globe 27 For additional information on confidence, see JP 2-0, Joint Intelligence. Expressing Confidence in Analytic Judgment to multiple end-users. Dissemination is accomplished through various means, each requiring continuous management (e.g., electronic transmission, hardcopy annotated imagery and maps, direct threat warnings, oral and written reports, briefings, and various servers allowing structured discovery and retrieval). When managed ineffectively, communications paths can become saturated by information retransmitted by numerous intermediate collection agencies, resulting in circular reporting. Advances in cyberspace capabilities and technologies should be leveraged to improve dissemination and reduce the information-to production timeline for delivery of intelligence products.

chunk_text: This access optimizes intelligence support to operations planning, preparation, execution, and assessment functions. Common Intelligence Feed/Picture (CIF/P): A flexible display of relevant, instructive, and contextual intelligence information regarding adversary, and neutral force disposition, and supporting infrastructures derived from all sources at any level of classification, shared by more than one command, which facilitates collaborative planning and assists all echelons to enhance situational awareness and decision-making. The CIP/F represents the synthesized product of the entire intelligence enterprise. It is the authoritative, shared source of ground truth about the adversary, flowing up from the tactical edge and back down from the strategic level. This critical warfighting function is executed at each echelon: within the AOC, the ISRD develops and maintains the operational level picture, supported by the DGS. At the tactical level, the CIC is responsible for maintaining the intelligence picture for its wing, ensuring immediate relevance to aircrew and combat operations.28

chunk_text: All operations in the intelligence process are interrelated and should be evaluated to determine the degree to which they facilitate each other and succeed in meeting intelligence requirements. Importantly, evaluation and feedback are not an independent operation in the intelligence process. Rather, it should be conducted continuously to assess the intelligence process. The goal is to identify issues as early as possible to minimize information gaps, mitigate capability shortfalls, and ensure intelligence operations meet established analysis tradecraft and objectivity standards. Evaluation and feedback require a collaborative dialogue between intelligence planners, 28 For additional information on the CIP/F, see the Air Land See Space Application (ALSSA) Center CIP/F Tactical Bulletin (TACBUL). collection managers, collectors, and analysts to identify deficiencies within the intelligence process. Assessment of intelligence operations relies on product satisfaction, as determined by the user. As such, intelligence personnel and users should provide timely feedback regarding intelligence process successes and problems.

chunk_text: As such, intelligence personnel and users should provide timely feedback regarding intelligence process successes and problems. A formalized process should be established to ensure feedback is provided consistently and analyzed systematically. Assessment Methods. An ISR strategy is not complete unless it includes a qualitative and quantitative ISR assessment. Assessments provide leaders with information to reprioritize intelligence requirements, shift collection emphasis, change analytic levels of effort, and reallocate available ISR assets. To assess intelligence activities and operations, intelligence planners develop and document intelligence measures of performance (MOPs) and intelligence measures of effectiveness (MOEs). • Measurement of Performance (MOP). An indicator used to measure a friendly action that is tied to measuring task accomplishment. MOPs are informed by quantitative indicators. They indicate whether a particular platform or sensor is performing as expected: e.g., the number of sorties conducted or number of images taken. • Measurement of Effectiveness (MOE).
